title: Auteur
subtitle: Director-by-director filmographies. Sections by auteur, films within in approximate viewing-order priority.

sections.name: I. Ingmar Bergman
sections.desc: Swedish. The most acclaimed filmmaker of post-war European cinema.

items.title: The Seventh Seal
items.whyPriority: Best entry point. Knight + Death + chess + plague.
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Foreign.

critics.quote: Special Jury Prize.

items.title: Wild Strawberries
items.whyPriority: His most accessible great film. Aging professor confronts his life.
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Foreign.

critics.quote: Berlin Golden Bear.

items.title: Persona
items.whyPriority: Bergman at his most experimental. Two women — actress and nurse — merge identities. The film school masterclass.
items.pitch: An actress stops speaking. Her nurse cares for her at a remote cottage. The boundary between them dissolves.

critics.quote: Routinely cited as Bergman's masterpiece. Sight & Sound canonical.

items.title: Cries and Whispers
items.pitch: Three sisters at a country estate as one of them dies. Bergman's most painful film.

critics.quote: Five Oscar nominations including Best Picture, Best Director.

items.title: Fanny and Alexander
items.whyPriority: Bergman's farewell. Warmer than most of his catalog. Watch the 312-min TV version if you commit.
items.pitch: An early-1900s Swedish family across years. Wealth, marriage, theater, magic, religion.

critics.quote: Won 4 Oscars including Best Foreign Language Film, Cinematography, Art Direction, Costume Design.

items.title: Scenes from a Marriage
items.pitch: A marriage across years, fights, separation, return. The TV version is the one to watch.

critics.quote: Cited by many as the most honest depiction of marriage ever filmed.

items.title: Winter Light
items.pitch: A pastor losing his faith during one Sunday's services. Sparse, brutal.

items.title: Through a Glass Darkly
items.pitch: A young woman recovering from mental illness during a family vacation. Won Best Foreign Language Film.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film.

sections.name: II. Akira Kurosawa
sections.desc: Japanese. The most internationally influential Asian director.

items.title: Seven Samurai
items.whyPriority: Best entry. Three-and-a-half hours that fly by.
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Foreign.

items.title: Rashomon
items.whyPriority: His international breakthrough. The film that gave us the word 'rashomon' for contradictory testimonies.
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Foreign.

critics.quote: Won Golden Lion at Venice.

items.title: Ikiru
items.whyPriority: A bureaucrat with cancer tries to do one meaningful thing before dying. Many call it Kurosawa's most emotional film.
items.pitch: An aging Tokyo civil servant, told he has months to live, tries to make his life mean something.

critics.quote: Routinely cited as among Kurosawa's masterpieces. Watanabe Takashi's central performance is iconic.

items.title: Yojimbo
items.whyPriority: Toshirō Mifune as the wandering ronin. The original A Fistful of Dollars (Leone remade it as a Western, illegally).
items.pitch: A masterless samurai arrives in a town divided between two warring gangs. He plays them against each other.

critics.quote: Mifune Best Actor at Venice. Foundational influence on Westerns.

items.title: Sanjuro
items.pitch: Yojimbo sequel. Lighter, looser. The blood-spurt at the climax has been imitated for 60 years.

items.title: Throne of Blood
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Drama. Macbeth in feudal Japan.

items.title: Ran
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Drama. King Lear, late Kurosawa.

items.title: The Hidden Fortress
items.pitch: Adventure film told from peasants' perspective. Lucas cited it as a structural model for Star Wars (R2-D2/C-3PO are direct descendants).

critics.quote: Berlin Silver Bear for Best Director.

items.title: High and Low
items.pitch: Modern setting. A wealthy executive's chauffeur's son is mistakenly kidnapped. Cops procedural in second half.

critics.quote: Routinely cited as one of Kurosawa's greatest non-period films.

items.title: 8½
items.whyPriority: Best entry. The film about being unable to make a film.
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Foreign.

items.title: La Strada
items.whyPriority: Fellini's most heartbreaking. Giulietta Masina as a simple-minded young woman sold to a brutish strongman. Won Best Foreign Language Film.
items.pitch: Postwar Italy. A traveling strongman buys a young woman from her impoverished mother. Their road life.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: Nights of Cabiria
items.pitch: Masina as a Roman prostitute who keeps believing in love. The final shot is one of cinema's most famous.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: La Dolce Vita
items.whyPriority: Marcello Mastroianni drifting through a week of Roman society. Anita Ekberg in the Trevi Fountain. The word 'paparazzi' came from a character's name.
items.pitch: A gossip journalist drifts through seven nights of Roman parties, encounters, and emptiness.

critics.quote: Palme d'Or.

items.title: Amarcord
items.pitch: Fellini's most autobiographical. A year in 1930s Rimini under Mussolini. Comic, nostalgic, sad.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: Andrei Rublev
items.whyPriority: His masterpiece. Three-and-a-half hours.
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Foreign.

items.title: Stalker
items.whyPriority: Already on your sci-fi tracker.
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Films.

items.title: Solaris
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Films.

items.title: Mirror
items.whyPriority: His most personal and most difficult. Memory, his mother, Russian history. Often cited as his greatest film by other directors.
items.pitch: A dying poet's memories of childhood, his mother, war, and Russia.

critics.quote: Frequently named the greatest film ever made by other filmmakers.

items.title: Nostalghia
items.pitch: A Russian poet researches an exile in Italy. His own homesickness becomes the film's spiritual subject.

critics.quote: Tarkovsky shared the Best Director prize.

items.title: The Sacrifice
items.pitch: Tarkovsky's final film. Made while he was dying. A man bargains with God to prevent nuclear war.

critics.quote: Grand Prix and Critics' Prize.

sections.name: V. Denis Villeneuve
sections.desc: Canadian. The most acclaimed working sci-fi filmmaker.

items.title: Dune: Part One
items.pitch: You've already loved this. Pre-listed.

items.title: Dune: Part Two
items.pitch: You've already loved this. Pre-listed.

items.title: Arrival
items.whyPriority: The film that proved Villeneuve could do thoughtful sci-fi. Adams's career-best. Whether you agreed with your mixed Arrival reaction or not, it's a foundational text for modern cerebral sci-fi.
items.pitch: A linguist is recruited to communicate with newly arrived alien spacecraft.

critics.quote: Eight Oscar nominations including Best Picture, Best Director.

items.title: Blade Runner 2049
items.whyPriority: Sequel to a film you've watched. Roger Deakins's Oscar-winning cinematography. Slower, more contemplative than the original — which may suit you better.
items.pitch: Decades after the original. A blade runner uncovers a secret with implications for the entire society.

critics.quote: Won 2 Oscars including Best Cinematography (Deakins). Five total nominations.

items.title: Sicario
items.whyPriority: Cartel thriller that's smarter than the genre. Blunt, Del Toro, Brolin. Deakins again. Taylor Sheridan screenplay.
items.pitch: An idealistic FBI agent is recruited into a black-ops task force on the Mexican border.

critics.quote: Three Oscar nominations.

items.title: Prisoners
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Crime.

items.title: Enemy
items.pitch: Adapted from Saramago. Gyllenhaal plays a man who discovers his exact double. Eerie, dream-logic, ends with one of cinema's most disturbing images.

critics.quote: Polarizing on release; reputation has grown. Cult standing.

items.title: Incendies
items.pitch: Twins travel to the Middle East to investigate their dead mother's past. Brutal, structurally devastating.

critics.quote: Best Foreign Language Film nomination.

sections.name: VI. David Lynch
sections.desc: American. The most singular sensibility in cinema.

items.title: Mulholland Drive
items.whyPriority: His masterpiece. Best entry to Lynch.
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Crime.

critics.quote: #8 in Greatest Films of All Time.

items.title: Blue Velvet
items.whyPriority: His breakthrough. American suburb's underbelly. Hopper at his most terrifying.
items.pitch: A young man finds a severed ear in a field. The investigation reveals what's underneath his small town.

critics.quote: Best Director nomination.

items.title: Twin Peaks: Fire Walk With Me
items.whyPriority: The Twin Peaks prequel film. Booed at Cannes; rehabilitated as one of his masterpieces. Sheryl Lee's central performance is staggering.
items.pitch: The last seven days of Laura Palmer's life.

critics.quote: Critically reviled in 1992; now widely cited as one of the greatest works of the 1990s.

items.title: The Elephant Man
items.whyPriority: His most accessible. Hurt's performance under prosthetics is among the great screen performances.
items.pitch: Joseph Merrick in Victorian London. Black and white, sympathetic, beautiful.

critics.quote: Eight Oscar nominations including Best Picture, Best Director.

items.title: Eraserhead
items.pitch: His debut. Industrial nightmare, deformed baby, anxiety as form. Watch only when you're ready.

critics.quote: Immediate cult phenomenon. Routinely cited as one of cinema's strangest masterpieces.

items.title: Lost Highway
items.pitch: A jazz musician is accused of murdering his wife. Then becomes someone else. Lynch at his most fragmented.

items.title: Inland Empire
items.pitch: Three hours, shot on consumer video. Laura Dern in his most experimental film. For Lynch completists only.

sections.name: VII. Paul Thomas Anderson
sections.desc: American. The most acclaimed working American auteur.

items.title: There Will Be Blood
items.whyPriority: His masterpiece. Best entry.
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Drama.

items.title: The Master
items.whyPriority: Phoenix and Hoffman. A WWII veteran falls in with a charismatic cult leader. Three Oscar acting nominations from one film.
items.pitch: 1950s. A drifting WWII vet joins a self-help movement led by a charismatic founder. The relationship between them is the film.

critics.quote: Three Oscar acting nominations.

items.title: Magnolia
items.whyPriority: His ensemble masterpiece. Multiple LA stories converge across one day. Cruise's career-best performance. The frogs.
items.pitch: Multiple LA characters across one biblically strange day.

critics.quote: Three Oscar nominations including Best Original Screenplay. Berlin Golden Bear.

items.title: Boogie Nights
items.whyPriority: His breakthrough. Late-70s/early-80s San Fernando Valley porn industry. Wahlberg, Reynolds, Moore. Tracking shots.
items.pitch: A young busboy is discovered and inducted into the porn industry. He becomes a star. Then the 80s arrive.

critics.quote: Three Oscar nominations.

items.title: Phantom Thread
items.whyPriority: Day-Lewis's final performance. 1950s London couture house. Vicky Krieps is electric. Subtler comedy than people initially noticed.
items.pitch: A renowned dressmaker meets a young waitress. Their relationship is more peculiar than either expects.

critics.quote: Six Oscar nominations including Best Picture, Best Director, Best Actor.

items.title: Punch-Drunk Love
items.pitch: Adam Sandler in his only great dramatic performance. A fragile man falls in love. Strange, brittle, sweet.

critics.quote: Won Best Director.

items.title: Inherent Vice
items.pitch: Cross-listed from Crime.

items.title: Licorice Pizza
items.pitch: 70s San Fernando Valley coming-of-age. Looser than his other recent work.

critics.quote: Three Oscar nominations including Best Picture, Best Director.

sections.name: VIII. Coen Brothers
sections.desc: Cross-referenced with comedy and crime catalogs. Listed here in full.

items.title: Fargo
items.pitch: Cross-listed from comedy.

items.title: No Country for Old Men
items.pitch: Cross-listed from drama.

items.title: The Big Lebowski
items.pitch: Cross-listed from comedy.

items.title: Inside Llewyn Davis
items.whyPriority: Their most melancholy film. 1961 Greenwich Village folk scene. Oscar Isaac's career-launching performance.
items.pitch: A struggling folk singer in early-1960s New York drifts toward final failure.

critics.quote: Grand Prix.

items.title: A Serious Man
items.whyPriority: Their most personal and most Jewish. 1967 Minnesota physics professor whose life unravels. Job in suburban America.
items.pitch: Larry Gopnik's life falls apart. He visits three rabbis. None of them help.

critics.quote: Two Oscar nominations including Best Picture.

items.title: Miller's Crossing
items.whyPriority: Their best gangster film. Prohibition-era Irish/Italian war. Gabriel Byrne's career-best.
items.pitch: An advisor to an Irish mob boss navigates a war with the Italians. Loyalty, betrayal, ethics.

critics.quote: Initially modest performance; critical reputation has grown enormously.

items.title: Barton Fink
items.pitch: 1941 Hollywood. A New York playwright takes a screenwriting job. Things go strange.

critics.quote: Palme d'Or — only Coens win, and a record three major prizes.

items.title: Blood Simple
items.pitch: Their debut. Texas noir. A husband, his wife's affair, a hired killer who has his own ideas.

critics.quote: Sundance Grand Jury Prize. Established their career immediately.

items.title: True Grit
items.pitch: Cross-listed from drama.

items.title: The Witch
items.pitch: Cross-listed from horror.

items.title: The Lighthouse
items.pitch: Cross-listed from horror.

items.title: The Northman
items.whyPriority: Eggers' Viking revenge epic. The Hamlet source story (Amleth) treated with full mythological commitment. Skarsgård, Kidman, Dafoe, Björk.
items.pitch: Tenth-century Iceland. A young prince, exiled, sets out as an adult to avenge his father's murder.

critics.quote: Mixed commercial performance; critical reputation has grown. Considered the most committed mainstream Viking film.

items.title: Nosferatu
items.pitch: You've already loved this.
